source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)
revision_note: Newly added with 8 verified chunks under public domain (2026-07-23).

content.text: Tegh Bahadur spent twenty years in quiet contemplation in a cellar at Bakala, until he was revealed to the public by Makhan Shah Lubana who cried, "Guru Ladho re" (The Guru has been found), acknowledging his spiritual path.
content.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

content.text: When Kashmiri Pandits sought the Guru's protection from forced conversion, the Guru told them: "Tell the Emperor that if he converts Guru Tegh Bahadur, you will all follow him," defending their freedom of religion.
content.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

content.text: Knowing the certainty of death, the Guru traveled to Delhi to meet the Emperor, offering himself as a sacrifice to protect the liberty of conscience of another faith.
content.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

content.text: The Guru's close disciples Bhai Mati Das (sawn alive), Bhai Sati Das (burnt alive), and Bhai Dayala (boiled in water) were executed before him, yet they all died with the Guru's hymns on their lips.
content.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

content.text: Refusing to show a miracle or convert, the Guru was beheaded in Delhi in 1675, showing that life is fleeting but truth and honor are eternal.
content.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

content.text: In his hymns of detachment (Salok Mahalla 9), the Guru taught: "Fear not, nor frighten anyone" (Bhai Kahu Kau Det Neh, Neh Bhai Manat Aan), as the key to spiritual liberation.
content.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

content.text: The sacrifice of Guru Tegh Bahadur galvanized the Sikh community, leading his son Guru Gobind Singh to establish the Khalsa to fight oppression and secure human rights.
content.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)

content.text: This entry compiles the historical accounts and translations from Max Arthur Macauliffe's "The Sikh Religion", Vol. 4 (1909), which is in the public domain and hosted on sacred-texts.com.
content.source_name: The Sikh Religion, Vol. 4, translated by Max Arthur Macauliffe (1909)
